title: Warning Chain Test
desc: The eruption alarms only matter if every bell and chain still works before the pond wakes up angry.

steps.text:
Lava Pond has calm periods and active periods. The difference between them is why the alarm chains exist. Bells and strike plates are mounted across the chamber so a watcher on one side can warn crews on the other before an eruption column turns the whole shelf line into a memory.

A calm morning is the right time to test them, which means it is also the right time for everyone nearby to be irritable about the noise. The alarm keeper drags a strike hammer along the chain rail and gives you a look that suggests failure would be more offensive than the racket. "Take the lower chain and make sure the bell line carries," she says, "or work the upper relay plates and verify the echo sequence. If one section dies, people die louder."

She puts a wax plug in each ear and offers you the same courtesy.

options.label: Test the lower alarm chain and bell line
Check the upper relay plates and echo sequence

steps.text:
The lower chain runs along the work ledges where crews can grab and yank it if the pond starts to rise. You move from segment to segment, pulling each length and waiting for the corresponding bell to answer from farther along the chamber. Most reply at once - harsh, metallic, impossible to ignore. One does not.

You follow the dead section to a pulley assembly clogged with volcanic grit. The keeper swears in a way that suggests this pulley has wasted her time before. Together you clear the grit, oil the axle, and reset the chain tension until it moves freely again. The next pull sends the bell crashing out over the pond hard enough to startle a whole shelf crew into glaring at you from across the lava.

"Excellent," the keeper says, entirely unsympathetic to their feelings. "Now if the chamber tries to kill them this afternoon, they'll have no excuse not to hear about it."

options.label: Make the final pull and sign off the lower line

steps.text:
The upper relay plates are for distance and confusion: broad bronze discs mounted high enough that one strike can skip the warning from one end of the chamber to the other in a distinct pattern. You work the sequence with the keeper, who strikes the first plate while you listen for the response downstream and mark the delay in chalk on the wall.

Two plates ring clean. The third gives a dull note and swallows the rest of the pattern. Up close, the problem is obvious - one mounting bolt has loosened, damping the vibration into the wall behind it. You tighten the bracket, strike again, and this time the sound leaps correctly from plate to plate, rolling down the chamber like a metallic wave.

The keeper counts the echoes under her breath, then nods when the last one answers back. "There. Predictable panic restored." She says it with satisfaction. At Lava Pond, dependable warning is as close as anyone gets to comfort.

options.label: Lower the hammer and record the sequence as clear
